title: Adiabatic work: the same area, a steeper curve, a cooler gas
scenario: A diatomic gas ($\gamma = 1.4$) expands from $V_1 = 1$ L at $P_1 = 100$ kPa to $V_2 = 3$ L with **no heat exchanged** ($PV^\gamma = $ const). How much work does it do, and what happens to its temperature? The work is again the area under the $P$–$V$ curve — but the adiabat falls off faster than an isotherm, so the gas does less work and *cools*. Drag the cursor out along the adiabat to watch the work accumulate; raise the initial pressure $P_1$ to lift the whole curve while its shape (and the cooling ratio) stays fixed by $\gamma$.

assumptions.claim: Quasi-static, reversible adiabatic process: no heat crosses the boundary ($Q = 0$), so $PV^\gamma = $ const holds at every step.
Ideal gas with constant $\gamma$ over the temperature range (no vibrational modes switching on).

steps.label: What the algebra-based course hands you (a formula to memorize)
steps.prose: For an adiabatic process the algebra course hands you $W = (P_1V_1 - P_2V_2)/(\gamma-1)$ with no account of where the $\gamma$ or the denominator come from. It can compute the number but cannot show that the work is the area under a particular curve — or why that area is smaller than the isothermal one.

isobaric_rectangle.label: If pressure stayed at $P_1$: $W = P_1\,\Delta V$ (the rectangle)

steps.label: Work is the area under the pressure–volume curve
steps.prose: As before, the work done by the gas is the integral of pressure over volume — the area under the $P$–$V$ curve. What changes from the isotherm is only the *shape* of $P(V)$.

steps.label: Use the adiabatic law $PV^\gamma = \text{const}$
steps.prose: With no heat exchanged, $PV^\gamma$ is constant, so $P\propto V^{-\gamma}$ — a steeper fall than the isotherm's $V^{-1}$. Integrating that power law gives the memorized adiabatic work exactly; its slope at any volume is $P(V)$. Because the curve drops faster, the area is *smaller* than the isothermal area between the same volumes.

steps.label: No heat in, so the gas cools
steps.prose: Since no heat enters, the work comes out of the gas's internal energy, so its temperature falls — combine $PV^\gamma=\text{const}$ with $PV = nRT$ and the temperature ratio is $(V_1/V_2)^{\gamma-1}$. The isotherm holds $T$ fixed by drawing heat from a reservoir; the adiabat has no reservoir, so the area you can extract is bounded by the cooling. $P_1$ sets the curve's height, but $\gamma$ alone fixes its shape and this cooling ratio.

proof.heading: The adiabatic work is the area under a steeper P–V curve — and the memorized result falls out, with the gas cooling.
proof.detail: check $\tfrac{dW}{dV} = P$; check $W = \int_{V_1}^{V} P\,dV'$; recover $W = (P_1V_1 - P_2V_2)/(\gamma-1)$; collapse the constant-pressure case to $P\,\Delta V$

checks.claim: The work's slope is the pressure: $\tfrac{dW}{dV} = P$ — the area's rate of growth is the curve's height.
The accumulated work is the area: $W(V) = \int_{V_1}^{V} P\,dV'$.
The memorized adiabatic work $W = \dfrac{P_1V_1 - P_2V_2}{\gamma-1}$ is exactly the area at $V_2$.
At constant pressure the integral collapses to $W = P\,\Delta V$ — the area is a rectangle (the quadrature).

misconception.claim: An expanding gas always does the same work for a given change in volume.
misconception.refuted_by: Work is the *area* under the $P$–$V$ curve, and the path sets the curve. An adiabat ($P\propto V^{-\gamma}$) falls faster than an isotherm ($P\propto V^{-1}$), so for the same expansion it encloses less area and does less work. And because no heat flows in, that work is paid out of the gas's internal energy — so the gas cools, $T_2/T_1 = (V_1/V_2)^{\gamma-1}$. Same volume change, different area, different work.
